channel.title: NYT > Business > Energy & Environment
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump’s Budget May Target Climate Programs. Here’s What Survived the Last Cuts.
item.description: Efforts to study, prepare for and address climate change have taken a major hit. But a few programs are hanging on thanks to bipartisan support.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Congress has prevented the dismantling of popular programs like Energy Star and the Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program.

item.title: ‘God Squad’ Waives Environmental Rules for Drilling in the Gulf of Mexico
item.description: The panel voted to override Endangered Species Act restrictions on oil and gas activities in the Gulf of Mexico, home to critically endangered whales and other imperiled wildlife.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Carolyn Van Houten for The New York Times
item.media:description: Demonstrators outside the Interior Department in Washington on Tuesday.

item.title: Edward Russo Says Donald Trump Is an ‘Environmental Hero’
item.description: Edward Russo, the only member of a White House task force, thinks the president doesn’t get enough credit for conservation at his golf courses, among other things.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times

item.title: Vermont Hits Back at Trump’s Effort to Block ‘Climate Superfund’ Law
item.description: The law would make fuel companies help pay for damages caused by climate change. The administration argues it’s unconstitutional.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Caleb Kenna for The New York Times
item.media:description: The federal courthouse in Rutland, Vt., on Monday, where the Justice Department was suing to block the state’s superfund law.

item.title: As Trump Squeezes Cuba, U.S. Military Exists in a Bubble
item.description: The Navy base at Guantánamo Bay sparkles at night while neighbors across a Cuban minefield struggle in darkness driven by a Trump fuel embargo.
item.dc:creator: Carol Rosenberg
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: A bowling alley at the Guantánamo Bay military base on a Saturday. Life on the base has been mostly unaffected by the energy crisis in Cuba.

item.title: Why the Iran War May Force Countries to Rely Less on Natural Gas
item.description: The U.S. and other exporters are poised for a windfall, but disruptions to Persian Gulf supplies are also pushing gas-buying countries to consider alternatives like coal, solar and nuclear energy.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Brad Plumer
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: A liquefied natural gas plant in Cameron, La., last year. The United States, the world’s biggest gas exporter, will almost certainly benefit from shortages caused by the war in Iran.

item.title: Clean Energy Companies Are Trying to Survive the Trump Era
item.description: Offshore wind is out. Geothermal power is in. And many climate technology start-ups are looking for ways to carry on without federal backing.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.media:description: A tour of the Geothermal House of the CERAWeek energy conference in Houston this week. Geothermal energy is emerging as one of the big green winners of the current era.

item.title: He Helped Write the Clean Air Act. He Fears for Its Future.
item.description: Thomas Jorling, adviser to Republicans who cosponsored the 1970 law, disputes the Trump administration’s claim that it shouldn’t apply to planet-warming greenhouse gases.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Cindy Schultz for The New York Times

item.title: Iran Moves to Formalize Toll Plan in Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Tehran has effectively closed off the critical waterway, turning back container ships on Friday, and Iranian lawmakers are considering whether to formalize charging fees to pass.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Iran has effectively cut off traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, leaving thousands of vessels idling on either side of it.

item.title: Japan and the U.S. Agree to Team Up on Seabed Mining
item.description: The arrangement could signal a fracture in the decades-long effort among nations to reach consensus on how to mine the ocean floor while protecting ecosystems.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:description: A Japanese seabed mining research vessel in Shimizu, Japan, in January.

item.title: Wealthy Investors Target Foes of Clean Energy, Seeking Revenge
item.description: Renewable energy leaders said their industry got “rolled” in President Trump’s tax bill. Now they’re fighting back, starting in Texas.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman and Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Mason Trinca for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Roseland solar and storage facility in Riesel, Texas. The state ranks first in wind and second in solar capacity in the country, generating nearly $300 million in state and local taxes annually.

item.title: Schumer Pledges Democrats Will Restore Clean Energy Tax Credits
item.description: Senator Chuck Schumer of New York, the minority leader, said Democrats would pursue an agenda to reduce energy costs if they win back control of Congress.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Chuck Schumer, the Senate minority leader, at the Capitol this week.

item.title: Trump Draws Bipartisan Backlash for Easing Oil Sanctions on Russia and Iran
item.description: Republicans and Democrats alike have criticized the Trump administration’s moves, taken to stabilize oil markets rocked by the war with Iran, warning that it is benefiting two U.S. adversaries.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A tanker carrying Russian oil in Gujarat, India, earlier this month. The Trump administration has defended the lifting of restrictions on Russia and Iran as necessary to prevent further spikes in energy prices that could ripple through the global economy and hit American consumers.

item.title: E.P.A. Waives Smog Rules on Summer Gasoline in Bid to Ease Prices
item.description: The moves will allow expanded sales of a higher-ethanol blend known as E15, which is often restricted in the summer.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer and Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An ethanol plant in Rochelle, Ill. The gasoline blend E15 — which consists of 15 percent ethanol — is restricted in the summer because it evaporates more easily in the heat, leading to smog and decreased efficiency.

item.title: As Gas Prices Spike, California Is Hit Hardest
item.description: A gallon has climbed to nearly $6 in the state, about $2 above the national average.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn and Kurtis Lee
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A gas station in Los Angeles this month. Critics of oil companies that operate in California say refiners have taken advantage of crises like the war with Iran to raise prices.

item.title: Maryland Supreme Court Strikes Down Local Climate Suit Against Big Oil
item.description: The decision represents a setback to other local governments around the country that have sued oil companies to recoup the mounting costs of climate change.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:description: Crude oil storage in Baltimore. The governments of Baltimore, Annapolis and Anne Arundel County had sued 26 oil and gas companies to recover damages caused by greenhouse gas emissions.

item.title: High Oil and Gas Prices Could Outlast Trump’s War With Iran
item.description: While the president has promised rapid relief, Americans could feel the financial sting of the conflict for some time after it ends.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Even when the conflict ends, it may be weeks or months before American families and businesses see a true break in spiraling energy costs.

item.title: Russian Oil Shipment Puts Focus on Kremlin Spy Outpost in Cuba
item.description: Moscow may be challenging President Trump’s effort to choke Cuba’s economy. China also has suspected listening posts on the island.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Mr. Trump has spoken of using his economic leverage to mount a “friendly takeover” of Cuba and is pressing for the ouster of its president, Miguel Díaz-Canel.

item.title: For Western Oil Companies, War in Iran Means Bigger Profits, and Risks
item.description: American and European oil and gas companies are expected to earn a lot more as prices surge but are worried about the future.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Storage tanks in Stanton, Texas. Companies that operate in places like West Texas are earning a lot more for each barrel of oil they produce.

item.title: Carbon Capture Technology Is Helping This Pub Make Beer
item.description: A pub in California is pulling carbon dioxide from the air to carbonate pints. If the business model works, it could give the broader carbon-capture industry a boost.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Nuwer and Ian C. Bates
item.media:credit: Ian C. Bates for The New York Times
